steps.title: Gas Pressure and the Particle Model
steps.content:
Gas pressure is caused by particles **colliding with the walls** of a container.

**Increasing temperature** → particles move faster → more frequent and more forceful collisions → **higher pressure** (at constant volume).

**Decreasing volume** → particles collide more frequently → **higher pressure** (at constant temperature).

At **absolute zero** (0 K = −273 °C), particle movement stops. Temperature in Kelvin:

T(K) = T(°C) + 273

steps.title: Gas Pressure
steps.question: A gas is heated at constant volume. What happens to the pressure?
steps.correctFeedback: Correct! Heating the gas makes particles move faster, so they collide with the walls more frequently and with greater force — increasing pressure.
steps.misconceptionFeedback: At constant volume: heating → faster particles → more collisions with walls → greater force per collision → higher pressure. P/T = constant (P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂) where T is in Kelvin.
steps.bestSource: AQA GCSE Physics — Particle Model of Matter
